body.text: State is the set of variables that describe what is happening right now. Every interactive program has state. A game tracks score, level, and whose turn it is. State changes when something happens, and behavior depends on what state it is in.

rows: Which cells the game lit up this round
Which cells the player has clicked so far
Whether the player can click right now
How many cells are shown this round

body.label: Key question
body.text: If you forgot to reset playerClicks at the start of each round, what would happen by round 3? Think through it before reading on.

By round 3, playerClicks would still contain clicks from rounds 1 and 2. The comparison would fail immediately because the array is already too long. Resetting state at the start of each round is not optional — it is required.

exercises.title: Trace state through rounds
tasks: Read the starter code. It simulates two rounds of the game without any DOM.
Before running: trace through it manually and write what litCells and playerClicks contain after each round.
Run it and compare the logged output to your prediction.
Change round 2 so the player gets it wrong. What should happen to level?
exercises.hint: Follow the assignments line by line. Each startRound() call resets playerClicks to [].

exercises.title: Boolean input control
tasks: Declare a variable called isInputAllowed set to false.
Write a function called handleClick() that logs 'Click registered' only when isInputAllowed is true, and 'Input is blocked' when false.
Call handleClick() three times. Then set isInputAllowed to true and call it three more times.
Challenge: write a function called toggleInput() that flips isInputAllowed between true and false each time it is called.
exercises.hint: Check the boolean with: if (!isInputAllowed) { ... }. To toggle: isInputAllowed = !isInputAllowed.

checks.label: handleClick() exists and can be called
